A PERSONAL WORD TO PATIENTS
Let’s keep this just between us, but if someone had offered this
book to m

one had offered this
book to me a few years ago, I would have run away screaming—if
not on the outside, then certainly inside of me. I admit that this
would not have been the best

would not have been the best response given the situation, but hey,
it was not unusual for me at that time to react differently from how
I should have done. Back then, I felt like

e done. Back then, I felt like I was constantly pushing a
wheelbarrow full of frogs down a bumpy, winding road. The frogs
would not stay put; they croaked at the slightest movement

aked at the slightest movement and
made the wheelbarrow lean over dangerously whenever I
encountered a bump or a pothole on my way. It often took more
strength than I thought I had

strength than I thought I had to keep this wheelbarrow upright and
to continue down the road.
The worst part, however, was that I didn’t see anyone else
struggling with such a pec

lse
struggling with such a peculiar load. I was ashamed of my
wheelbarrow and did my best to make sure that nobody would
notice it. After all, what could I answer if someone were t

uld I answer if someone were to ask me
how I came by so many frogs? To be honest, I hardly knew most of
my frogs. I thought of them as green monsters and regarded them
as no more t

and regarded them
as no more than a burden that I had to bear. I did not even know
how or when some of them had climbed into my wheelbarrow—or
why.
Then one day, when I arrived at

en one day, when I arrived at the biggest, deepest pothole in
the road, I realized that I could no longer do this alone and that I
needed help from someone else to keep the wheelba

meone else to keep the wheelbarrow upright on
this stretch of the road. It took a lot of courage and a great deal of
trust to dare ask for that help—trust in that other person, but

rust in that other person, but even
more, genuine trust in myself. I needed to believe that I could ask
for such help, that I would not helplessly collapse if someone cast
an eye o

lapse if someone cast
an eye on my many frogs, and that together we would find a way
past, through, or over the biggest obstacles.
Trust is an important theme in this book. Again a

nt theme in this book. Again and again, you
will read that you and all of your parts must learn to rely on each
other, that it is important to connect with people you can count on,

with people you can count on,

and that you can be sure of the support of your therapist. Of course,
it is not at all easy to have or to feel such trust. However, that may
be the

ust. However, that may
be the most important (and most beautiful) lesson you can learn
from this book: that there are people you can trust and that you are
worthy of letting yourse

u are
worthy of letting yourself be seen by them.
The authors of this book are such people. When you read what
they have written, you will find that they have your very best
intere

hey have your very best
interests at heart. They possess the knowledge, the experience, and
the empathic skills to help you. I know, because I have worked
through each and every to

rked
through each and every topic in this book as part of my own
process. Suzette Boon, Kathy Steele, and Onno van der Hart know
what they are talking about. When I look back on th

about. When I look back on the years I
struggled with the topics they discuss in this book, I can only say
that what they write is true. And, much more important, it helps.
I know

re important, it helps.
I know my frogs now. If anything, they have turned from green
monsters into dear green friends. They sit still when I want them to
—and if the path becomes

m to
—and if the path becomes somewhat treacherous again at times,
they are very willing to lend a hand and help push my
wheelbarrow. They have considerably diminished in number, b

erably diminished in number, by
the way, but that doesn’t even seem important anymore. Together,
the load was a lot easier to carry anyway.
In order to come to this point, I have h

o come to this point, I have had to learn many new
skills. All of those skills are included in this book. I did not write
the book, of course. Still, it feels like it’s about me. T

it feels like it’s about me. That is
probably exactly why I would have run away if someone had given
it to me at the beginning of my therapy: It named that which I did
not want to

that which I did
not want to have named and it showed what I preferred never to

eact differently from how I should have done. With the wisdom

If this were indeed my book, I would dedicate it to

book, I would dedicate it to my therapist
—for the way she taught me how to trust her and for the trust she

your therapist. Now all you need to do is to take a deep breath, turn

lieve in all
your parts, and believe in your therapist.
I know you can. I trust in you.

Introduction
A daily and weekly structure, with a balan

weekly structure, with a balanced distribution of work,

activity, and leisure, is of great importance for everyone. A
satisfying life includes knowing how to enjoy working but als

g how to enjoy working but also how
to enjoy and use to best advantage your leisure or free time.
Everyone does best with a daily structure that is consistent, but not
too rigid. S

nsistent, but not
too rigid. Structure helps people keep track of time and of what
they are doing, so they can be more attentive and able to
concentrate, and less worried or confus

te, and less worried or confused about what comes next.
Structure may help reduce the risk of intrusion of, or switching
among, parts of the personality; it may also help reduce th

ty; it may also help reduce the risk of
prolonged flashbacks or sinking into depression. Everyone has the
need to start and complete certain tasks in a timely manner, to
manage a h

a timely manner, to
manage a household, and to make relatively balanced choices about
how he or she spends free time.
Daily and weekly structures should include a regular time to g

ld include a regular time to get
up and go to bed, regular and healthy meals, necessary chores (for
instance, shopping, cooking, paying bills, cleaning), time for
relationships and

g), time for
relationships and social contacts, personal (“me”) time, inner
check-ins, physical exercise, fun, and other safely stimulating
activities, and so forth. In this chapte

, and so forth. In this chapter we will focus on tools for
establishing a daily structure, including keeping track of time,
healthy work habits, and using your leisure time to best

sing your leisure time to best advantage.

Introduction
The instability experienced by people with a complex d

ced by people with a complex dissociative

disorder is often related to the reliving of past traumatizing events,
also known as flashbacks or reactivated traumatic memories (Boon,

ted traumatic memories (Boon,
2003; Van der Hart et al., 1992). In this chapter you will learn
more about traumatic memories and how they are different from
everyday, normal memori

t from
everyday, normal memories; why they are so overwhelming; and
how they are related to dissociative parts of yourself. Traumatic
memories are often evoked or triggered by a st

en evoked or triggered by a stimulus (trigger) in
the present that is a reminder of some aspect of the original
traumatizing event. You will learn more about triggers and how to
re

e about triggers and how to
recognize them. In the next chapter, you will then learn how to
reduce and cope with triggers.
Autobiographical Memory and Traumatic Memory
Generally pe

Traumatic Memory
Generally people are able to recall important events they have
experienced in the past. They realize the event has happened to
them and that it is not happening n

and that it is not happening now. The memory is, so to speak,
a part of their “autobiography.” But as you may painfully
experience at times, this is not the case with traumatic mem

ot the case with traumatic memories.
When you, or a dissociative part of you, reexperiences a traumatic
memory, you feel as though it (or at least some aspect of it) is
happening i

e aspect of it) is
happening in the present.
Traumatic memories may include intense or overwhelming
feelings, such as panic, rage, shame, loss, guilt, despair; conflicting
beliefs

despair; conflicting
beliefs and thoughts; physical sensations such as pain; visual
images, sounds, and smells; and also behaviors, such as running
away, fighting, freezing, or sh

way, fighting, freezing, or shutting down. Each of these aspects of
a traumatic memory can occur simultaneously, in succession, or
separately at different times. Typically, these r

rent times. Typically, these reactions are not
appropriate to the current situation, or they are far more intense
than the situation warrants. You may want to return to chapter 4 f

want to return to chapter 4 for
a review of traumatic memories (flashbacks).
Understanding Triggers
A trigger (or reactivating stimulus) is something that bears a literal
or symbo

that bears a literal
or symbolic similarity to an aspect of an unresolved traumatic
experience. It may be a present-day situation, an interaction with
another person, an object, o

frightening emotion. People who have been seriously traumatized,

th past anger

Decision making is fundamental to all of our actions. We make

However, some people, particularly many traumatized individuals,

the near and dis

Basic Assertiveness Skills
Assertive behavior offers people a number of important benefits.
Assertive people tend to have fewer relatio

ple tend to have fewer relational conflicts, and thus
less overall stress. And because they have fewer conflicts, assertive
people often have more stable and strong relationships w

ble and strong relationships with others.
They get their needs met more consistently, and they can help
others get their needs met as well, which further strengthens
relationships.

her strengthens
relationships.

skills. You mu

Healthy Boundaries
Healthy boundaries help you protect and take care of yourself.
They can be set respectfully, but firmly: Boundaries a

ully, but firmly: Boundaries are not threats.
They are clear communications about your limits and the
consequences of crossing your limits when a person continues to do
so. The oth

so. The other person has a choice about whether to continue his or

the other person’s behav

the other person’s behavior. Boundaries most often have to do with

about money, that is, how finances are managed, how much money

es are managed, how much money
you are willing to spend, and how much you are willing for your
partner to spend.
Some people may be confused about whether setting boundaries

ther setting boundaries

oundary,

ning course. Furthermore, they should be

Introduction
Saying goodbye, or leave taking, is an inevitable

eave taking, is an inevitable and normal part of
life. But precisely because it is unavoidable, it is important to learn
how to have a good experience in saying goodbye to someone

in saying goodbye to someone else.

Yet many people avoid it as much as possible because it does
sometimes involve painful, sad, or disappointed feelings. You have
been working ha

ings. You have
been working hard to improve your ability to tolerate feelings and
decrease your avoidance, so hopefully you are in a better position
to cope with what comes up for

o cope with what comes up for you as you say goodbye to the
participants and trainers. As you come to the end of this course, be
sure to use the skills you have learned to help you

s you have learned to help yourself. The
importance of leave taking is discussed next.
Saying Goodbye
It is time to say goodbye. You have come to know other people in
this group, s

other people in
this group, some more and some less, but nevertheless, you have
been united together in your struggle with difficult problems, with
your suffering, and with your i

our suffering, and with your intentions to work toward healing.
These are important commonalities, even though you each may be
quite different from the other in many ways. You have

e other in many ways. You have risked
sharing a bit more of yourselves with each other—not an easy feat!
This has created a bond among you, even though you may have had
conflicts o

h you may have had
conflicts or disagreements with each other from time to time. That
is normal and part of growing relationships. You have worked hard
to understand and empathize

d

congratulate yourselves on this important work that you have
accomplished as a group. And your sense of being connected wi

ur sense of being connected with
each other makes saying goodbye difficult.
The aim of this course was for you to learn new skills and
information that will help you cope better in

problems more adaptively, and to develop more inner empathy,
communication, and cooperation. But we also hope you have

e best, you likely learned some helpful

We congratulate you for the hard work you have done over the
course of this training. Please do not be discouraged if you did not

u did not

be chronic and can take awhile to resolve. But each of

awhile to resolve. But each of us has seen

st on your continued
